title: 2025/11/20 - ENV (Part 1 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-11-19 - Environment Committee - Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill [Part 1]
description: Thursday, 20th November 2025 | Environment Committee Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Royal Forest and Bird Society of New Zealand Richard Capie, Chief Advisor - Policy and Advocacy Peter Anderson, Legal Advisor New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Daniel Jackson, Member Sarah Shaw, Member
descriptionSummary: Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill. Royal Forest and Bird Society of New Zealand. Richard Capie, Chief Advisor - Policy and Advocacy

chunks.text: Morning

Session now, uh, and hearing submissions today on the Fast Track approvals Amendment Bill, uh, and first up, today we have Forest and Ver welcome Richard to the Select Committee. Good morning this morning. Uh, you are with us for 20 minutes this morning, so, uh, take your submission as read. Of course, uh, members will have questions, but we'll hand over to you for your opening remarks.

Uh, good morning. Thank you, Madam Chair. Uh, Tana Carto, Carto, everybody. Uh, and thank you for this opportunity to speak. Uh, my name is Richard Kappy. I'm Chief advisor at Forest and Bird, and fingers crossed joining me online, uh, is, uh, Peter Anson, uh, barrister, uh, who has been advising Forres and Bird on a number of the fast track applications that are currently in train.

Since the Fast Track Approvals Act came into force, Forrest and Bird has been engaged constructively in a number of significant fast track projects. We have experience from the front line and several decisions. Fast track panels themselves have explicitly acknowledged the value that organizations like Forest and Bird are bringing to the process.

These examples demonstrate that their independent expertise improves outcomes, reduces environmental risk, and actually increases public trust. Against this background, it is extremely concerning that the Amendment Bill would effectively shut out such advice from future fast Track decision if passed.

This bill removes the ability for panels to provide or invite in input from experts, NGOs, or community groups. Unless councils decline to comment. It blocks invited experts and NGOs from appealing clear legal errors. It allows applicants to influence who sits on panels deciding their applications, and it concentrates decision making in the hands of ministers.

It also collectively reduces legal oversight and public transparency. These are very material decisions that will lead to poor decision making and greater long-term risk to communities, ecosystems, and the crown. Before I talk a little bit more in detail about our concerns, I'd like to make some comments about the process that this legislation has been following.

Uh, as you know, this bill began, uh, with a bit of a narrow kind of punt around grocery competition. I note the gross the term grocery is mentioned once in substantive clauses, specifically Clause 59. The bill itself focuses on the fast track process as a whole, uh, with the specific policy for supermarket competition outlined separately, I would suggest that if the government really wishes to improve grocery competition, they should focus legislation to achieve that purpose.

Instead, this bill made without public input and without a regulatory impact assessment significantly alters New Zealander's Democratic rights to participate in decision making. And bear in mind that these are, by their very nature, under the original legislation nationally and regionally significant, these are landscaping community altering decisions that will last generations.

There was no analysis of these changes in the related treasury briefings, yet they have major implications for public participation. Ian Harley writes, environmental safeguards and administrative Fairness. The amounts of time provided to Parliament and the New Zealand public to consider and comment on legislation of this important and magnitude is totally unacceptable.

The number of submissions on the original legislation clearly show the level of interest and show the strength of feeling around fast track. Uh, IMY Sins was one of 27,000 people who marched down Queen Street in protest against the original legislation. The level of interest in this bill is very significant. The amount of time that people have got to comment on it and participate in this process is negligible.

Returning to the draft legislation itself, a critical concern for forest and bird is the concentration of powers in the hands of ministers. The bill would allow ministers to issue government policy statement defining what counts as quote unquote regional or national benefit panels would be required to consider these statements.

Something that in effect, let's minister predetermine the benefits of a project, while panels will then only be responsible for assessing the environmental costs. This goes against the very logic that this committee and Parliament relied on when shifting decision making from ministers to independent panels last year.

These ministerial statements require no public input, require consultation only with ministers whose portfolios match the subject matter, excluding environmental ministers significantly. They also give ministers disproportionate control over panel outcomes. To all intents and purposes, they vest ministers with the ability to pick winners and greenlight projects.

The risks flagged during the original legislation remain removing transparency and openness, undermining checks and balances on executive power, and furthering open further opening up the prospect of lobbying and conflicts of interest. Forest and bird strongly recommends removing these clauses.

While this proposed change is perhaps the most concerning. There are other changes in this propo in this bill that are perhaps just as bad. The bill removes the requirements to consult council's, agencies, iwi and hapu before lodging applications. This is inconsistent with Titi and risks reducing Maori involvement in decisions affecting reducing com comment timeframes to 20 day from 20 days to 15 days, makes it harder for agencies with statutory environmental responsibilities to provide robust evidence-based advice.

Again, thinking about the nature of these projects, it feels like we are trying to shoot ourselves in the foot by reducing the amount of time to gather and collect evidence to make good decisions. The bill denies NGOs and community groups, the appeal ability to appeal on legal errors, even when a panel invites their input.

Errors of law will instead be pushed into judicial review, which is slower, more expensive and counterproductive. So if the intent behind this bill is to speed things up, uh, I think you need to think again. Requiring potential panel members names to be given to applicants invites panel shopping and actually fundamentally undermines the independence of decision makers.

This is most egregious, allowing ministers to change schedule two by order of council is constitutionally inappropriate and bypasses parliamentary scrutiny on project scope, allowing applicants to substantially modify an application with no opportunity for further expert input risks panels approving projects never considered at the referral stage.

So taken together these changes, silence independent environmental expertise, reduce transparency, diminish democratic participation, increase legal and environmental risk. Weak can checks on ministerial and applicant's applicant influence, undermine public confidence in environmental decision making, and ultimately, they will lead to worse decision making.

You don't improve the ability to get things right for massive, complex projects like a new tunnel through the middle of Wellington or a new hydro dam by putting in place arbitrary and unrealistic timeframes and limiting your own ability to seek information. Do we really think it only requires three times as long to consider an application for a new Mount Victoria Tunnel, as it does to consider an application for a new house on Mount Victoria?

This is not a minor technical amendment. It is a significant weakening of public and environmental safeguards and will lead to worse decision making. Accordingly, forest and Bird asked the committee to remove provisions, enabling government policy statements on benefits, remove restrictions on panels, inviting expert comment, preserve full rights of appeal on points of law, retain meaningful consultation requirements with councils, iwi and affected parties, remove provisions, allowing applicants to influence panel appointments.

Remove the ability to amend Schedule two via order in council, allow panels flexibility in decision timeframes, and provide further comment when applicants are materially modified. Finally, I'd conclude by noting Forrest and Bird has proven that constructive expert public input improves decisions and reduces environmental harm.

The member Bill would reduce our, remove that voice. We urge the committee to protect transparency, democratic rights, and the health of our environment by rejecting the most damaging provisions of this bill. Failing to do so will make this law unsupportable and remove any semblance of legitimacy.

This, in turn, significantly increases the justification and likelihood that decisions made under this law will be revisited and won't be recognized by a future government. Nahed Nui, uh, for Bird would welcome the opportunity to answer your questions.

Thank you. Thank you, Richard, for that very passionate, uh, insight and submission. Look, we've got a couple of questions, so we will move to Celia Wade Brown. First, Um, thank you and really appreciate, um, not just the passion, but the depth in which you've actually looked at this bill, given the very short time that it's been around.

And I would like you to enlarge on how in 0.5 you say that it would exclude Forest and Bird and presumably many other organizations on all future fast tracked applications. Could you, it's not just to do with grocery shops and, um, we'd all like, um, a little bit more competition there, but what, what, uh, why, why do you think there's absolutely no limitation on which projects Forest and Bird would be excluded from?

So, I'll, I'll just comment. Um, the nature of the bill is, is not about grocery competition. It is about fundamentally amending the fast track legislation and the changes would apply to all future projects. Um, Pete, I'll hand over to you 'cause you've been actively involved in, uh, a number of the actual projects on the ground. So maybe you could give a bit of color to actually what that involvement and panel processes looks like.

Um, yeah, sure. Um, if you want, yeah, so, um, just to, to answer that question that the, um, the, the currently for Berg gets invited to comment on a number of these, uh, applications, and it's done so, and it's done so, um, in a way that has been recognized by panels as being constructed. Then, um, and useful, the the way in which that, that will, um, proceed in future is that that will only happen if there's no one else around, um, the council so that they, that they'll be dealing with the issue.

So effectively it, it removes, um, that, that voice from the, you know, the NGO or or other potential comments, it's not just forest and Bird that this apply to from being able to participate in the process and that that voice is, um, is often the only voice that's going to going to be advocating for a certain, um, uh, outcome.

And a good example is that was the genesis, um, fast track scheme, which for was invited to comment and effectively was, um, the lone voice in relation to certain parts of that. And the, the way in which these processes work best is where you have, um, it's a co it's a both two sides arguing their point and then the panel making a decision.

And if you don't have that, that second side, then the, the decision making is, is far, is far less robust. Thank you. Very clear. Thank you. Look, we'll move to Rachel Brooking now. Thank you. And related to that, uh, I know Forest and Bird is involved in a lot of resource management in Conservation Act and other, uh, issues.

And I wanted to talk about the scale of the projects because Richard, you said consenting a tunnel is different from consenting a house at Mount Victoria. And so is it forest and birds experience that all of the fast track projects that Forest and Bird has been involved in, or most of them, you tell me which one are of a scale that would normally end up in front of a judge at an environment court.

Uh, and then secondly, if you can also comment on the sorts of, um, evidence briefs that Forest and Bird has been giving. And I say that because you've just told us, uh, the Peter that, uh, you are only be invited to comment via panel with these changes if the council doesn't have expertise and it recognizes that it doesn't have expertise and asks for, um, forest and birds involvement.

And what sort of involvement, um, have you been giving to these fast track applications? I can, I can already answer that. Um, Richard, so, so the first, the first, um, the first answer is, um, the projects that we've been involved in have ranged from the, from the very significant.

So that would be the, the likes of trans resources, seabed Mining, and the, and the Taranaki bike to, to, um, to, to ones that are at the less, you know, of the scale of them. So, and, and would, would, would, would all of them end up in front of a court? Maybe, maybe not. Some of them might, some of them might not. That might not happen for some of the, the less controversial ones.

But, you know, the likes of trans resources, that's probably gonna always end up in front of a court because it's so, um, controversial. And I guess the, the, the notion that, that you can have a panel make sense for decision within 60 working days of receiving comments just seems, um, it seems, um, are totally implausible. Um, given, given the the breadth of, of complicated and difficult issues to resolve.

It's just going to result in, in poor decision making and, and decisions that are gonna be, um, able to be challenged because they, they haven't had time, the panels haven't had time to consider the issues, but they're forced to make a decision. So, so that, that's the, that's the first, um, that's the first point. Second point in relation to the expertise that's been, um, called forests called a range of expertise, um, in, in, in the, in the case of, I've been, been involved in two of them, um, which as tt r Genesis I said before,

and that's in involved calling ecological evidence from, um, from freshwater Ian, terrestrial, marine mammal, um, experts also calling, um, planning evidence and, um, and evidence on economics. So we've been kind of fully engaged in all the, in all the, all the disciplines that you would expect to arise in these kind of processes.

So just as supplementary to that, uh, those types of experts that you just mentioned, ecological and avian, what are they providing to the panels? How is that helpful and is, uh, consideration of those matters? Just stopping things from being built? No, so, so effectively that the, the evidence that's being called from the EC college is just to outline the impacts of the act that the activities will have, um, on those ecological features.

And, and, and that's, that's generally, um, there'll be some evidence from the, and evidence from those cases just provide, you know, different, different interpretations, um, have they have that resulted in things not happening. The Genesis ones controlled activity. So, um, so that, that was never gonna, you know, that has to be granted. So, um, no, that hasn't slowed down in transit, some resources.

Um, it's just, it's a large, it's a large project with a lot of, um, invited parties. So, um, engaging in one additional, um, party, which I think we call three breach of evidence in that planning, uh, and economics and marine mammals, it just adds, it adds to the quality of decision making, not to the length of time.

And so does it add to changes in consent conditions? Uh, hard, hard to tell, hard to tell at the moment. Um, I dunno, I, I, So typically in our resource management process, when you are providing information, it's one of the factors that gets picked up in the consenting decision.

So the better information you have, the, the more likely that a panel is able to make a good robust decision, which actually manages the effects of something that they've decided will go ahead. So this isn't just about stopping things, this is actually about improving the outcomes for the communities that are gonna be affected. The other thing is, as Pete said, if you've got a weak process, then it is more likely to end up in a legislative, um, uh, challenge.

So making sure that you've got a robust process in place where people have got time to gather the right evidence where the right evidence is brought forward, that actually reduces the, the risk of litigation. Um, so the changes have been proposed here are actually likely to make the, the risk of judicial review greater. So rather than trying to expedite a process, this, this is likely to have the opposite effect.

Thank you. Look, we'll move to Cameron Luxton for his question. Thank you. And Chair, uh, I've got a question, Richard, about one of the com uh, comments you made, uh, regarding grocery competition, um, requires specific legislation. Would you concur with many people who, uh, who, who say that the, the, one of the major issues with getting Go grocery competition is New Zealand's long consenting timeframes, uh, making overseas competitors unlikely to want to come to our far-flung part of the world and actually provide that grocery competition competition

that would drive down prices for New Zealanders when they're gonna the grocery store. Um, I, I don't think Forest and Bird has an issue about, uh, provisions being made to, uh, improve grocery competition. This bill is not about that. Um, so there are some provisions in this, but they're minor compared with the rest of the legislation. So Just to be clear, before moving back onto the rest of the legislation, you don't think that, uh, consenting pathways taking multiple years for grocery competitions to end, uh, competitors

to enter New Zealand market is a fact that grocery competition, Um, I, I think there's been a wealth of information written about the different issues behind, uh, the challenges faced in New Zealand around grocery competition. I think that's one of the areas that has been touched on. Thank you. Look, I'm just gonna ask the last question that we have time for, um, because you do talk about, um, the projects that you've already been involved in, um, and you say that you feel you've got better outcomes.

Can you give an exact example of, um, the, a project where you contributed to and what that outcome was that you felt was a better outcome? Sure. I I think probably the, the observation I'll make is that the, the five that we're involved in are still active. So I don't think any of them are completed at this point in time.

Pete, um, maybe you could add a bit of color to that in terms Yeah, no, it's probably too early in the process to, um, to, to actually be able to do that because the, the, um, the, uh, the ones that, yeah, they'll, they're still active in un and underweight, but Okay, you, because you have put that in your submission that, um, you know, obviously the contribution is adding to better outcomes, but are you saying it's just too early to tell us? Well, what, what, What, what we're saying is that the panels have been recognizing, recognizing the engagement and the,

and the value that Forest Bird has added to the process. But in terms of how, how that results in substantive outcomes, it's, it's kind of too early to, to actually say, or would, there's recognition there from decision makers that our, our engagement in the process is, is valued. I, I think a key point is that we, we respect the independence of the panels and their legitimacy.

What we are hearing back from them is their ability to get information from organizations like Forest and Bird is helping them in their deliberations and providing value. And I think that is the key point when it comes to this bill, is that this bill would close that down, and I think that is completely unnecessary and unwise. Thank you very much, Richard.

We will have to leave it there. We have met time. Uh, we appreciate your time today, uh, and your insightful submission, so thank you. Thank you much. Thanks, Sam. Thank you. Uh, Now we will move on to the New Zealand Law Society, uh, who I understand are joining us via Zoom online today.

Good morning. We can see you there. Welcome to the select committee this morning. You have 20 minutes with us. We'll hand over to you for introductions and, and members will have questions. Um, Kiara Coto. Um, I'm Daniel. I'm Daniel Jackson. And, um, I appear with, uh, uh, my colleague, uh, Sarah Shaw, um, on behalf of the New Zealand Law Society.

We are both members of its, um, environmental Law Committee. Uh, and, um, we'll, we'll just touch in our oral presentation on some of the major, um, concerns outlined in part A of the Law Society's, um, submission. I'll start and then hand over to Ms. Shaw.

Um, and so, uh, our, our first concern is around the, uh, rushed process, um, with the law and the limited, uh, consultation, uh, and engagement and the limited timeframe, um, for submissions. We think that this risks, um, producing, uh, a law that doesn't achieve its objectives and could prove unworkable in places.

Uh, and so, for instance, um, we have highlighted how the, uh, proposed restrictions on, on consultation by panels, um, could, could prove unworkable, uh, because they'd have to, um, check with local authorities, um, as to whether, uh, you know, whether they're planning to submit all particular issues, and then could only invite other parties if they, uh, thought if, if, if they considered that, that what the local authorities and statutory agencies would be providing would be insufficient.

Um, and that the point, the point that we make there is that at this stage of the process, it's very likely that the local authorities, um, and statute authorities will not have decided, uh, what they're going to say in their submissions or comments. Uh, and therefore it's going to be impossible for the panel to make that sort of informed decision.

Um, we're also concerned about the workability in relation to timeframes, which I'll come onto in a moment. Um, the second point is around the ministerial powers. So the bill, uh, would allow the minister to issue a government policy statement, um, regarding, uh, uh, what, what co in essence, what constitutes regional, uh, or national, uh, benefits of, um, the significant regional national benefits.

Uh, we think that there needs to be some safeguards there. Um, there's no public consultation process for that as there are for government policy statements under, uh, a number of other statutes. Um, and we think that there need to be some, some sort of, uh, uh, guidance on, on what can be included in that.

Uh, so for example, providing that a government policy statement can't seek to enable specific projects, um, and requiring consultation, um, and, uh, uh, following the provisions, um, uh, the approach of the, of existing provisions in the Crown Entities Act.

Um, so for, so, uh, actually, sorry, that that's in relation also to the, um, to the proposed power to, to issue, um, directions to the EPA over, um, over how it, uh, exercises its functions under the act. Uh, that, that's the other ministerial power that we have concern about, because in, in essence, the, the EPAs per performing, um, independent functions, uh, statutory functions as the administering agency under it, and we think that there need to be provisions, uh, like in the Crown intersect that make clear that the minister, um,

can't direct a specific action in regard to a, to a statutorily independent function as opposed to setting general expectations on the agency. Um, and the final concern that I want to note is around the reductions in timeframes. Um, so the bill provides for shorter timeframes at several stages of the process, including requiring the panel convener to set up a panel within 15 working days of receiving a substantive application, uh, and also capping the timeframe for a panel to issue its decision at 60 working days, um,

after the deadline for comments unless the applicant agrees otherwise. Uh, we think that that's going to be, in some cases, unworkable, uh, for both of those. Um, the, and on average, it's taken 20 to 30 working days so far for the panel convener to set up a panel. Um, they have to find su uh, panel members with suitable expertise are available, which isn't always easy.

Um, and this timeframe has allowed them to conference with key participants to enable, uh, informed and efficient procedural decisions. And that often, um, pays dividends down the process. Um, and we also think that the 60 working days period, um, will just prove unrealistic, particularly in, uh, highly complex applications.

And the timeframes and the legislation already put panels, applicants and other participants under great time pressure. And so our recommendation is that in both cases, the panel convener needs to have the ability to set a longer timeframe, um, where it wouldn't be practicable to meet the timeframes that are proposed from the bill on this point.
Um, and it, and I'll hand over now to Ms. Shaw, um, to speak to the other, uh, concerns. Are you able to see us, uh, Sarah, I think you, you might have your video off.

Here we go. Good morning. So the, uh, the second two points are, um, first is the right of appeal. So rights of appeal are narrowed under the bill, excluding a number of parties from appeal, including anyone additional from whom the panel has chosen to invite to submit.

And that's recognizing that the ability to invite comment, uh, is also substantially narrowed under the bill. The Law society has significant concerns about this reduction in appeal rights, uh, both relating to national natural justice and also because, uh, the law society considers that it will not have the intended effect.

An era of law that could have been raised under appeal can also be raised by judicial review with the high court. And so establishing, um, a framework through these amendments where the concerns are likely to be actually drawn out through two separate processes simultaneously, uh, is likely to result in greater procedural complexity, greater inefficiency and additional expense for the applicant.

And this is an example which illustrates the law society's wider concern that the proposals in the bill, uh, reduced consultation constrained timeframes, limited rights with appeal will ultimately prove counterproductive, uh, in, in addition to raising issues of natural justice, uh, that could perversely undermine the efficiency and effectiveness that's been sought through the fast track process itself and the government's intentions for the bill.

And the last point was around transitional provisions. The bill contains none, which is a significant emission. If transitional provisions are not included, it'll be extremely challenging for users of the legislation, and there is a real risk that it could lead to mitigation. The orthodox position is that legislative amendments do not apply.

Retrospectively media reports have noted minister's expectations that these changes would apply to projects already under consideration as long as the decision had not yet been issued. If this is the intention, any proposal to apply those amendments to existing applications would need specific transitional provisions.

There would, however, be significant complexity in that approach and in law society's view, little gained in seeking to apply those processes to current applications. So the simplest and fairest solution is the orthodox position that the amendments would not apply retrospectively. So in effect, anything that had already, um, been submitted before the bill receives ascent, uh, the provisions would not apply.

Alternatively, um, the new processes could take effect, uh, but only if applications had not yet, um, gone to a panel, that would be, um, the other way of dealing with it. Um, or they could go to this, the remaining parts, but the suggestion that the, um, decision could have to be reworked based on the new provisions is unworkable in the new society section.

Thank, thank, uh, look, we moved to, uh, from Rachel. Yeah. Uh, and in interesting there that you are making the same point as forest and bird, that in fact, this, these changes could lead to slowing down things because decisions won't be made properly and there'll be more scope for judicial review.

Um, I'm interested in the comments and your experience with, uh, environment courts and hearings and, and other processes around the suitability of the people on the panel and what sort of expertise you're talking about. So I'm not so interested in the, uh, maybe the smaller scale projects, but the big complex projects.

What sort of expertise would you expect to see on these panels, regardless of the, if they're a fast track project or if they're going to an environment court or a special hearings body, independent hearings panel type process? And how does that expertise that a decision maker might have help with or benefit the process and your experience?
And how many, and the, the secondary question is, um, how many of those types of people exist in New Zealand?

Oh, can you hear hear That? Yes. Yes. Um, so the, the types of expertise depends on the nature of the project. And so the question is in terms of across the environment court, uh, the traditional council hearing processes and fast tracks. So the environment court, uh, typically sits as a panel of three with a presiding judge.

And, um, and for a typical application, there are two of the environment commissioners who are assigned those environment commissioners have expertise across, uh, engineering, landscape ecology planning, um, among other, um, so among other, so the, the panel for an environment called the bench for an environment court hearing is hand selected for the nature of the application, and the bench can be larger than the typical three if they need to add additional expertise, uh, for council hearings, um, the largest panels can be, um, I'm,

I'm chairing one of, of nine. Uh, the typical council panels, um, unless it's sole, um, chairperson are typically, uh, at least three, there can be five. The expertise, again, comes from a range of commissioner experience across, uh, relevant to the application. So it could be ecology, landscape, stormwater, heritage, uh, the expertise is, is hand selected.

The benefits of that expertise to the process is to be able to interrogate the evidence that is presented. So it doesn't matter which forum, uh, it's to engage with the expert evidence to, um, make findings on the evidence and to consider not just the approvals process, but also the appropriateness of conditions.

Um, so I think that answered all parts of the question. Just, just the last part of the question, and thank you for that answer, uh, was about how many of the, you've talked about people being handpicked, how, how much of that depth of expertise is in New Zealand and in terms of, uh, the timing you mentioned earlier, um, Daniel, about the, uh, the time it takes to set up a panel to work out who these, the right people are.

Daniel, did you wanna address that last part Of it? Yeah, um, I mean, certainly what we've heard is that, um, you know, in some cases it has been challenging to find, uh, people with suitable expertise and who are also available, um, in the relevant time period and don't have conflicts, um, for, uh, uh, to be on panels, particularly if you're looking for, uh, expertise in a specific area.

And there's actually a requirement proposed to be added in the bill to require panels, to have someone with expertise in the, essentially the, the subject matter area of the, um, application. So if you're looking for a specific, um, area expertise in a specific area, and you are also, um, needing someone who's available to do quite a lot of work fairly intensely in a particular timeframe and, um, doesn't have any confidence of interest, then that may require, you know, going to quite a number of people before you actually find, uh,

somebody who's, who's able to do it. And that all takes time, um, which is difficult if you've now got a very limited timeframe for setting up the panel. Thank you. Thank you, uh, Ryan, with the next question. Oh, thanks Chair. Uh, Sarah, in your, um, submission, you talked about how this proposed process is gonna streamline some of the, um, I guess processes and doing, doing them concurrently and the view that it will speed up and make it more efficient by up to six weeks.

Your argument was that it's potentially gonna, uh, make it more challenging and slow the process down and inefficient. So I was just wondering where that rationale is coming from. Thank you. Um, so the rationale for the, the submission that, um, counterintuitively the amendments could actually slow things down, uh, is that there are a number of, while there are a number of streamlining provisions added, there are actually also some that, um, will inherently add additional steps.

So for example, there's a proposal for the ability to contest, uh, the suitability of panel members, and there are, um, working days assigned for that process. Or in fact, there, there are no working days assigned. There's no time limit assigned for one of those parts of the process, which is for applicants to provide their comments.

So that's adding an additional step that is not currently, um, dealt with in that way. Currently, it's dealt with, um, through the convener calling a, um, a conference of the parties to discuss that in a, in a single, um, meeting. So other examples where things could end up taking longer is that if, uh, that the removal of consultation from the beginning of the process and replacement of that with notification.

Uh, generally in resource management circles, the earlier that issues can be flushed out and can be considered by an applicant, the better an application tends to be in its substance. So the replacement of consultation with notification early is something that could counterintuitively end up with more, um, time required later in the process to actually try and tease out what those issues are and for the applicant to then respond to them.

Uh, another one is the example that we've given where if the appeal rights are removed from people and uh, they take a judicial review instead, then those would be two separate high court processes rather than being joined as a single high court process. And then that those two high court processes are being run on completely separate timeframes and completely separate proceedings.

So those are three. Um, just, and earlier in your comments, you mentioned that one of the new things that's being proposed has no working days tagged to it, so it's a bit, I guess, ambiguous. But if we put working days on that, would that not then clarify or time that process It would for that particular steps.

So the law societies made specific recommendations in the table for a number of improvements that could be made if, uh, the provisions are gonna remain. Uh, but the point being that that entire process of contesting the appointment of panel members, um, is an additional step in the process that was not previously provided for.

Um, and there are other examples where, um, extra time is going to be, uh, taken to resolve some of these issues potentially, uh, where this, the system currently, uh, does provide flexibility for those to be, um, worked out before the application is lodged or through, uh, the tling of individual application.

Thank you. Q, See, can I, I like to ask to expand on section four where you talk about the unusually broad powers that this bill would give to ministers, and in particular whether, um, their ability to issue a government policy statement, um, almost about a particular project rather than a really broad policy statement, um, could open our resource management systems up to accusations of corruption.

Uh, well, I think our, our concern here is essentially that there is very little, uh, it's virtually nothing provided in the bill, uh, regarding what, what a government policy statement, uh, ca can do, uh, beyond simply the statement, uh, that it, that it would, uh, contain provisions regarding, uh, what qualifies as, uh, uh, regional or na significant regional and national benefits.

Uh, so it's essentially an open-ended discretion and we don't think it would be appropriate for those statements to be targeted at, um, particular projects because obviously, uh, there's supposed to be an independent process here and, uh, it, it would be inappropriate for the minister to be essentially saying to the panel who's making a decision on whether there's significant national or regional benefits, there's, that there's significant national, regional benefits, uh, for a particular project that would undermine the

independence of the decision making. So, so what we think is important, uh, is to, is for, to include some, some safeguards to ensure that the project, um, is that the power isn't, um, able to be used to make directions in relation to a particular pro, to a particular project, um, rather that it's supposed to be a more general power, um, in addition to our recommendation re re uh, regarding, uh, requiring consultation.

Thank you. Thank you. Just time for one last question from Grant Pellum. Yeah, very quickly, um, just you, you talk about the timeframes is obviously a bit of concern and particularly when referring to larger projects, 60 days is, might not be, um, long enough, I suppose, is what, what would you consider a more realistic timeframe for this, for the decision making process once you get to that point for, from particularly for more complex projects?

Well, I think, I think the point that we'd make is that it is quite dependent on the nature of the particular project. So, you know, some projects, 60 working days might be fine, uh, other, other day, uh, other projects, uh, really we think it's important that the convener has the ability to set a, um, a, a project, a a timeframe that's based on the, on the, on the particular project.

And we have seen projects where the, the timeframe that the applicant has in fact said, we think is, is, is reasonable, is, is more than, uh, 60 days because they recognize the complexity of it. So it, it, it does depend, you know, you're seeing some projects, um, where you know, it, it, it might be longer than that might be, say, a hundred working days.

But I think our position would be that rather than having a specific, you know, arbitrary cutoff, there needs to be some flexibility for the convener to set what is necessary for the particular project taking into account, for example, the volume of information, the complexity, the, the number of commenters and so on.
Thank you. Look, thank you very much. We have met time today. We appreciate your time very much. Your submission very thorough. So you have a good day. Thank you. Thank you.
